title: I Got SHADOWBANNED on YouTube To Prove It's Not luck

transcript.text: So, in this video, I'm opening a brand new channel, and I will go viral in 7 days. >> Within the next 30 days, I'm going to try posting on a brand new shorts account with the goal of getting it monetized. >> This is a YouTube channel I started just 10 days ago to buy my dream car. But to be honest, it was kind of easy. Mhm, yeah. If you're a YouTube creator and you've been looking to grow your own channel, but I'm sure through your research, you've come across these types of videos on a platform already. The ones that have titles along the lines of I did something incredible on YouTube in a very short space of time to prove it's not luck. And let me be clear, I'm not here to discredit any of these videos because if I'm honest, we've used the same title ourselves. But there is a harsh reality to all of this, isn't there? For the vast majority of creators, blowing up on YouTube just ain't going to happen. So, I thought to myself, what if I did the complete opposite? >> Okay, let's check out my analytics. And oh, there we are. One of 10 with zero views. That's right. What if I could get my channel shadow banned to prove it's not luck. So, in this video, I'll show you the channel I created, the videos I made, and the analytics that led to YouTube obliterating the reach of every single piece of content I tried to publish. But first, how do we get here? Well, it came from a request from our boss, who you may have seen on our YouTube channel audit live streams or other places on YouTube many, many years ago. Fixed camera somersault. Yeah. >> [laughter] >> Now, he thought it would be good idea to start YouTube all over again, a brand new channel completely from scratch to try and understand and empathize with the plight of the new creator on YouTube. Now, I'll be honest with you, I was just too damn busy to take this project seriously. So, I took the 2026 solution to this YouTube dilemma. AI slop. Page three says the dials go in first. >> No, that's page four you're looking upside down. >> I'm not upside down. The paper is >> pick a side and stop doing it? After all, a lot of these videos that prove it's not luck are based around generating AI shorts videos. And if this video about cats, which, if you remember, cats are stupid, got 41 million views, then a YouTube short about man's best friend is bound to do better, right? And with that, we already have the three major pillars of this YouTube channel solved. The next thing I had [music] to do is come up with a channel name. And since this channel was going to be all about the weird and wonderful life of dogs, I thought it would be a nice play on the term it's a dog's life. It's an AI dog's life, Jasper. You get it? Clearly not. Content, we need some for our new AI channel, and it's easier than ever to generate videos in 2026. And that's exactly what I did with a simple and single prompt. Pixar style animation dogs playing poker. All right, let's see what you mutts got. I'm raising. Bold move, short legs. I'll call that. >> Wow, that was surprisingly quite easy. I don't think I've ever made a video faster than that in my entire life. But to be honest, I'm not sure how I should feel about that as a creator on YouTube. Okay, remix this video. Same dogs at shopping sale. Check out that sign, 70% off. >> We're hitting the jackpot, pups. >> Extra 20 if we buy two? >> Done. Same dogs stuck in commuter traffic. I swear [music] we've moved 10 ft in the last 15 minutes. 10? You're being generous. Try five. Same dogs walking their own dogs. Yes. Never thought I'd be out here doing this on a Saturday morning. Walking a dog while you walk your dog? It's a workout and a half. >> Mhm, that's it. I can't really think of anything else that goes on in a dog's life. But since I am trying to outsource as much of this as possible to my good friend here, let's try this. The prompt to generate videos is same dogs playing poker. Come up with 50 more prompt ideas for the same dogs doing everyday activities. A few minutes later. Okay, let's see what we have here. Oh. Mhm, all right. Yeah. Mhm. Mhm. Let's get it quickly. Now, by accidental luck more than anything else, because I had remixed all of my videos from that one source video, the dogs had retained their character. And that meant I was able to get AI to generate a circular image with those dogs as my channel logo. And with that, we're just about ready to kick off with this thing. And that is when I bumped [music] into my first big problem. If you've just started a brand new YouTube channel, you need to know this. There is a limit on how many videos you can upload to your new channel in a 24-hour period. I just created a brand new channel and was uploading shorts when I hit the limit, which was 10 videos. Now, this channel was literally brand new, not even a day old, and it wasn't even verified. Yeah. I didn't think of that. Now, I did quickly realize that actually publishing 10 videos a day on this channel would be absolutely ludicrous. So, what I did is uploaded my 30 or so videos over a few days and then scheduled one video to go out per day with the same type of titling strategy. It's a dog's life {dot} {dot} {dot} whatever the dogs were doing, and use three really popular dog-related hashtags. So, I would say up to this point that the total time spent on the channel was maybe around about an hour, and that was mostly waiting around for videos to be generated. And I suppose at this point, it's important to remind ourselves of what the actual objective of this challenge is. It's not to go viral, it's not to get monetized, it's not to build a community. It's quite the opposite, in fact, to get as little views as possible and to find out whether YouTube actually does, quote unquote, shadow ban channels. And at first, that didn't actually happen. The first video published on the channel got a very respectable 2 and 1/2 thousand views, which quite frankly surprised me. And it continued like that for a couple more weeks. Some videos got a few hundred views, others around about [music] a thousand views, and people were actually subscribing to the channel. And it was very obvious from the moment the channel was launched who was its biggest fan. So, this initial success seemed to confirm two things. Yes, YouTube will give your brand new channel a chance to get discovered on the platform. 20,000 views in 3 weeks confirms that. And furthermore, because these are YouTube shorts, YouTube is pumping them through the shorts feed. And that means there are any impressions because there's no thumbnails or titles to click on. >> [music] >> So, instead, YouTube has to test new shorts with what we call a seed audience. That is around about 1,500 to 2,500 viewers. And this is why you'll typically see in the first 24 hours of YouTube shorts life this surge in views seems to come out of nowhere. It truly is the only way for brand new content from a brand new channel to be seen on YouTube. And quite astonishingly, when people did see a short from It's AI Dog Life and decided to stick around to watch it, they watched the entire thing and a little bit more. As any creator starting out on their YouTube journey, they would be delighted to see audience retention of above 100% for most of their initial shorts, right? Well, hang on, hang on. We're getting a little ahead of ourselves here. Isn't this all too good to be true? Yes, yes, it is. We need to remind ourselves of the type of content and the volume of content this channel is pushing out. >> Here comes the drop. Hold on to your ears. Tails in zero gravity. So, yeah, it does look as if YouTube is testing this content with thousands and thousands of people. And if you was decide to stick around and watch the short, they do stay until the end. But there is a critical word in that sentence that we haven't yet addressed. If. From day one and indeed video one, the It's AI Dog Life channel was doomed because of two critical data points we haven't yet dived into. The first one being engaged views. Because while indeed this short did get 2 and 1/2 thousand views in its first 24 hours, far more importantly, only 500 of them were classed as engaged views. And the best way to demonstrate what I'm talking about is to do this. It's clear at the speed that I'm skipping through these videos that I'm not watching them, right? I'm flicking through the shorts feed until I find something to watch. But despite the fact that these clearly aren't views as we would understand them, YouTube counts them as views. Look, all of these Z's in the real-time stats are a result of what I've just done. An engaged view, on the other hand, would look more like this. I'm watching it for a period of time, essentially [music] engaging with the content itself. And that engaged view metric leads us directly onto the next mission-critical data point, stayed to watch. This is set directly against swipe away. So, think of it as engaged views versus views in total. And for this first short on the channel, it got a stay to watch percentage of just 25%. I recommend, right now, if you make YouTube shorts to go look at that metric because I guarantee you it won't be as disgraceful as 25%. This is where I can explain the second part of that graph you saw earlier. Absolutely, YouTube is going to test your short with a seed audience to get the critical data it needs. From that data, it's going to take the appropriate action. And in In case, three out of every four viewers flicked through the short without a moment's notice, which told YouTube to Yeah, just kill it. And that's where we hit this, what is often referred to as a views ceiling. According to YouTube, over 20 million videos are uploaded to the platform every single day. And you can bet your bottom dollar that most of them are YouTube shorts. As a result of this, YouTube doesn't want to waste time with shorts that it's already tested with a seed audience that's reported back to the algorithm that they're not interested in the content. There are tons more shorts ready to serve up to viewers. And it worked all of this out within 24 hours of a video being published. On day one, the short got 2,400 views. Then, in the following 60 days, just 100 more. It would seem as though the YouTube algorithm is incredibly efficient or terrifyingly ruthless. Choose your point of view. But that is just video one. And we already know that it's AI dog life has at least one super fan. So, what about the other 30? Well, as mentioned earlier, the first few weeks did bring views. And as with any other channel, there was a bit of views variance between each short, but each one did get its shot in the shorts feed. But inevitably, due to the nature of each of these shorts, they had the same problem. Each and every one of these shorts performed in exactly the same way. The stay-to-watch number never climbed above 35% and tanked to as low as 15% at times. As a quick comparison, the stay-to-watch percentage for the main VidIQ channel is 70%, and the best shorts channels often see numbers north of 80%. And so, as predictably as night following day, the short would get its views often in the first 12 hours and then completely flatline thereafter. Now, if you've learned anything over the years here at VidIQ, it's consistency in all its forms rewards success. So, that can also work both ways. If you are consistently uploading AI slop that an audience clearly isn't interested in, something is eventually going to give. And the first red flag on the It's AI Dog Life channel came with the 17th video that got 16 views. And it got just five views in the first 24 hours, or 485 views less than typical, or just 1% of the views you would typically expect on this channel for a short in the first 24 hours. Now, I think we'll all agree that this doesn't feel like the typical peaks and troughs of video performance on a channel, does it? >> [snorts] >> Oh, yeah, that smells like a shadow ban to me. But hang on, hang on, we're not there just yet. A few more shorts afterwards returned to the typical numbers. But not long after that, the views really started to slide. And then, from the 26th video onwards, the It's AI Dog Life channel got well and truly savaged. Let me just read off the view counts for every short from here onwards. Six views, seven views, eight views, five views, three views, one view, three views, four views, three views, four views, six views, no views, two views, three views. And to be quite honest, many of those views will have come from me just casually swiping through the shorts around about five minutes ago. The channel seemed to go through three stages of life: the testing stage, the suspicious stage, and finally, the shadow ban stage, which it's been in ever since. And all of this has allowed me to enjoy a most unique YouTube experience. A few moments later. Okay, I think it's time to check the studio once again. Uh oh, look. There we are. I appear to have done it again. This is a literal cheat code to get one of 10 on your videos every single time. And I've proved it's not luck. So, to summarize my achievement here, I've basically managed to shadow ban a brand new YouTube channel in 25 days with 25 videos. And I did it by basically provoking the most dangerous word in the YouTube lexicon right now. Inauthentic content. It is content that is essentially made with a simple prompt template with little to no variation across videos that could easily be replicable at scale. This channel would absolutely, positively never get monetized for those reasons alone. But to be honest, it would never reach the monetization requirements of 10 million shorts views because it's been shadow banned. But let's look at it from this point of view. As YouTube creators, as viewers on the platform, we actually want the algorithm to do this, don't we? To identify trash content, AI slop, and given how the audiences reacted to the content so far, remove it from view. I know shadow banning, or shadow ban, is a filthy word at YouTube headquarters. But this test does seem to suggest that there is some sort of trigger, some sort of switch that suppresses the reach of content if the audience has already decided it's very unsatisfactory. And all of this leaves me with just one final task. Hello, everybody. This is Jasper, the mastermind behind It's AI Dog Life channel. And he would just like to apologize profusely for the aggravation he has caused to the YouTube users, YouTube creators, and YouTube themselves. He did not realize he was going to get his channel shadow banned so quickly. And he's just pleading with anybody out there who will listen to reinstate the algorithm to his content. Jasper, do you have any final words? Thank you. Look, Jasper, you got some views. You fixed it. You just don't care, do you?
